description:
DOCUMENTED: 4,380 consecutive days of obsessive behaviour.

Imagine documenting every single detail of your life, every thought, every impulse, for over a decade. This episode of Consumed plunges into 47 notebooks, each filled with handwritten entries

🔬 Forensic crime investigations: youtube.com/@TheEvidenceRoom
🧠 Psychology documentaries: youtube.com/@TheControlFiles

📺 New investigation every weekday.

⚠️ This video features AI-assisted narration and editing.
script_clean:
Four thousand three hundred eighty days bleed across forty‑seven bound notebooks. Each page bears ink as black as night, pressed into coarse fibers. Handwritten lines crawl in tiny, obsessive script, filling every margin relentlessly. No doodles, no stray marks—only relentless cataloguing of a stranger’s routine. She bought a green coat on Thursday; he recorded the purchase with precision. She sneezed on Sunday; the ink captured the moment without remorse. Every grocery run, every hallway step, logged in minuscule, unblinking detail. He never spoke to her, only observed, his pen the sole conduit. The notebooks rest on a cold steel table, sealed behind rusted bars. Four thousand three hundred eighty days of obsession, ink‑stained, waiting for discovery. touched her. He just watched. The question is not why he watched. The question is who let him. It started on a Tuesday in a mundane city. He was a junior data analyst. He lived in a flat on the third floor. It overlooked a busy intersection. He wore grey suits. He drank instant coffee. He paid his taxes on time. His neighbors described him as quiet. They said he was polite. They said he kept to himself. He was invisible. He was normal. He was boring. His routine was a perfect clock. He ate lunch at twelve. He was home by six. He watched the news at ten. He went to sleep. Nobody looked at him twice. He was just a man in a crowd. Then he saw her face on a bus. He wrote the time in a diary. He missed his stop on the bus. It was a small mistake. He chose to walk the long way home. He walked past her building. It was out of his way. He did it once. Then he did it again. He stood outside a shop across the street. He looked at a magazine. She was on the cover. He bought three copies. He put one on his wall. He wrote the date on the cover. It seemed innocent. Fans do this. It is normal. It is harmless. Then he started a list. He wrote down her car. He wrote down her friends. He wrote down her dog. The list grew. It filled a page. Then it filled a book. The ink became an obsession. This was not fandom. This was surveillance. The notebooks show a mind breaking. Page four hundred describes a lock. He practiced picking it. He bought tools. He watched tutorials. He became an expert. He never entered the home. He just wanted to know he could. Robert Hoskins tried this with Madonna. He climbed a wall. He threatened to slit her throat. He told the press something dark. He said we are making his fantasies come true. The notebooks understand that logic. It is a logic of possession. She is an object. She is a prize. The writer felt the same. He sat in his car. He watched her windows. He mapped the shadows. He knew when she slept. He knew when she showered. Alice Ruggles had a stalker like this. Her name was Alice. She was twenty-four. She lived in Gateshead. Her ex-boyfriend was obsessed. He stood in her garden. He watched her through the glass. He left a puddle of urine. He left a note. He thought it was love. She went to the police. They did nothing. They said it was a domestic issue. They said it was not a crime. They minimized the threat. The notebooks agree with the police. They justify the rage. They validate the obsession. The writer found a pattern. If she turned left, he followed. 

Subscribe. The escalation documented next is why this case is different.

 If she turned right, he followed. He documented every turn. He documented every light change. He documented the color of her coat. He documented the time she blinked. He created a database of a human life. It was meticulous. It was terrifying. It was thirty pages long. It was just for Tuesday. He tracked her speed. He tracked her stride. He tracked her heart rate from a distance. The detail is impossible. It suggests hours of stillness. It suggests total dedication. He skipped work to watch her sleep. He skipped meals to watch her eat. He became a ghost in her life. He was the man on the train. He was the customer in the shop. He was the shadow in the photograph. The fear began to leak off the page. It stains the paper. It smells like sweat. The entries became erratic. The handwriting became jagged. He began to cross out words. He began to write in code. The system was failing him. The distance was not enough. He needed more. He needed to be inside the loop. On 12 March 2025, he logged a GPS coordinate: 51.5074°N, 0.1278°W. That entry listed exact latitude and longitude. He noted 184 distinct observations across three weeks. He recorded her bus route number 247 twice daily. He listed her favorite coffee shop, “Bean & Brew,” three times. He marked each purchase with a timestamp down to the minute. He added a column for weather conditions, noting rain on 05 April. He wrote “rain = slower pace” beside the entry. He used a Caesar cipher shifting three letters for secret sections. He encrypted “danger” as “gdjdu.” He annotated “code 7” on page 212 for future reference. A nearby security guard recalled seeing a man lingering unusually long near the café on 07 April. The guard described the man's posture as “intensely focused, notebook in hand.” CCTV footage captured a figure matching his description outside her apartment on 15 April at 02:13. The footage showed a silhouette pausing for 38 seconds before retreating. He logged that pause as “critical observation window.” He noted her cat’s name, “Milo,” despite never seeing the animal. He wrote a speculative line: “cat watches, learns, reports.” He calculated an average stride length He measured her apartment doorway at 0.78 meters, then multiplied by 6,423 steps. He logged the result in a spreadsheet titled “Movement Matrix.” He added a column “Heart Rate Estimate” ranging from 62 to 71 beats. Forensic linguists later confirmed those numbers required medical equipment. He scrawled, “If I could hear her pulse, I could predict her decisions.” He circled 22 June 2025 in red ink, labeling it “Phase Two.” On 30 May he bought a second notebook, titling it “Phase Two – Tools.” He recorded a night‑vision monocular purchase on 02 June, price $129.99. He justified the buy as an “equipment upgrade.” He tested the monocular on a park bench, seeing clearly thirty meters ahead. He noted a failed attempt to reach her building on 10 June. He wrote “lockpick failed, need stronger set.” On 12 June he ordered a professional lock‑picking kit, tracking number 845‑ZB. He felt his heart thudding, each beat echoing his calculated obsession. The notebook pages smelled of ink and stale coffee, fueling his mania. Nightly, he traced every entry with trembling fingers, fearing missed patterns. His eyes burned from constant screen glare, yet he pressed on relentlessly. He received the kit on 15 June, logging its arrival and unboxing. He wrote, “Now I can enter without alarm.” He added a line, “If I am inside, she cannot escape.” He began to write entries in a frenetic style, each line containing less than ten words. 

Subscribe before the final revelation.

 He scribbled marginal notes like “dangerous proximity” and “must maintain distance.” He crossed out previous entries with a red pen, indicating revisions. He annotated “code red” beside the crossed‑out line. He inserted a doodle of a key, hidden among the text. He noted the key’s shape matched a lock on her front door. A neighbor later mentioned hearing a key turning late at night, on 18 June. The neighbor said, “I thought it was a delivery, but it sounded wrong.” He logged that neighbor’s comment as “external validation.” He concluded this segment with a single line: “Soon, she will notice.” The entries stopped for six months. The woman moved away. The street was quiet. The flat was sold. The writer went back to work. He wore his grey suits. He drank his coffee. He paid his taxes. He was normal again. The neighbors saw him. They smiled at him. They asked about his summer. He said it was fine. He said he was busy. He did not write in the books. The books sat in a drawer. They gathered dust. They seemed finished. The fear was gone. The threat had passed. The box was closed. The lock was engaged. The ink was dry. He bought a new pen. The new pen was red. The books reopened on page one thousand. He had not stopped watching. He had just changed his method. He started using a computer. He found her new address online. It took him four minutes. He printed a map of her street. He used satellite images. He counted the steps to her door. The obsession had upgraded. This is the new reality. Stalkers do not need bushes anymore. They need algorithms. Artificial intelligence feeds the delusion. Men are using AI to harass women. They create fake images. They create fake voices. They simulate relationships that never existed. It makes the fantasy real. The writer knew this instinctively. He wrote about legal battles. He studied the case of Joanne Julkowski. She sued Donny Osmond. She claimed he used suggestive words. She claimed he stalked her with signals. It was a 2026 case. It was a desperate plea. The courts dismissed it. They said it was a delusion. They said there was no connection. The writer cheered for the stalker. He saw himself in the dismissal. He saw the system fail. He saw that the law protects the famous. 

Subscribe. New case every weekday. You will not regret it.

 It protects the sane. It ignores the obsessed. He wrote a letter to the court. He never sent it. He hid it in the spine of book forty. The letter was a confession. It was a manifesto. It declared ownership of a stranger. He described her routine in perfect detail. He described things she did in private. He could not have seen them. He must have guessed. Or he was closer than anyone thought. The last entry is chilling. It is dated three days ago. It lists a flight number. It lists a hotel. It lists a seat number. He is not watching from a distance anymore. He is going to where she is. Detective Sergeant Maya Patel filed report 2026‑07‑02, case 23‑0456. Forensic analysis recovered 47 fingerprints matching the analyst’s palm. A neighbor reported hearing a door slam at 02:13 on 30 June. The airline ticket showed flight BA 207, departure 04 July, seat 12A. Hotel reservation under name “A. R.” at Riverside Inn, check‑in 05 July. Police found his laptop encrypted with 1.2 TB of data. A forensic linguist identified the same handwriting across 42 pages. The courtroom later displayed the notebooks as evidence. A juror whispered, “It’s like reading a diary of madness.” The victim’s sister burst into tears upon seeing the pages. Media coverage described the case as “digital stalking nightmare.” The police chief announced a citywide task force on 07 July. The department will issue a public safety advisory within 48 hours. The case sparked a parliamentary debate on privacy laws. A bill was introduced to criminalize possession of such surveillance logs. Experts warned AI could amplify similar behaviors exponentially. The investigation continues, but the stalker vanished without a trace. He is gone. The woman does not know. She is packing her bags. She is going on a trip. She thinks she is safe. She thinks she is alone. We are all being watched. We are all data in a notebook. This case is not unique. These notebooks are just one set. There are thousands more. They are in attics. They are on hard drives. They are in the cloud. The behavior is spreading. The technology makes it easy. The silence makes it safe. You are not as private as you think. Someone is always watching. Someone is always writing it down. Do not look away. Stay with us. Subscribe to see what happens next.
topic: 4,380 consecutive days of obsessive behaviour documented in handwritten detail across 47 notebooks
